stem: A team wants coding conventions to be shared with everyone who clones the repository and applied automatically in every session. Where should these conventions live?

options.A: In project-level CLAUDE.md (root or .claude/CLAUDE.md), committed to version control
options.B: In each developer's user-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md
options.C: Pasted into the system prompt at the start of every session
options.D: In a .claude/skills/ skill that each developer invokes manually

explanation: Project-level CLAUDE.md (root CLAUDE.md or .claude/CLAUDE.md) is committed to version control, so it is shared with the whole team and always loaded into context. This is the canonical place for shared, always-on conventions.

distractors.B: User-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md is personal and NOT shared via version control, so teammates would not get the conventions.
distractors.C: Manually pasting into the system prompt is not shared, not version-controlled, and error-prone.
distractors.D: Skills require on-demand manual invocation; they are not always loaded, so conventions would be applied inconsistently.

stem: A developer wants to keep a few personal Claude Code preferences (preferred commit message style, verbosity) that should NOT affect teammates. Where should these go?

options.A: A directory-level CLAUDE.md in the repo's src/ folder
options.B: A .claude/rules/ file with a broad glob pattern
options.C: Root CLAUDE.md committed to the repo
options.D: User-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md

explanation: User-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md holds personal preferences that apply across all of a developer's projects and are never shared via version control, making it the correct home for individual, non-team settings.

distractors.A: A directory CLAUDE.md inside the repo is still committed and shared with teammates.
distractors.B: A .claude/rules/ file in the repo is shared and scoped by file path, not by individual user.
distractors.C: Root CLAUDE.md is committed and shared, so personal preferences would leak to the whole team.

stem: Your CI job runs `claude "Review this diff for security issues"` and the build hangs until it times out. What is the correct fix?

options.A: Set a shorter session timeout in the CI configuration
options.B: Wrap the command so it pipes 'yes' into Claude Code's stdin
options.C: Add the --interactive=false flag
options.D: Add the -p (print) flag: claude -p "Review this diff for security issues"

explanation: The -p (or --print) flag runs Claude Code in non-interactive mode: it processes the prompt, writes output to stdout, and exits. Without it, Claude Code waits for interactive input and hangs in CI.

distractors.A: A shorter timeout just makes the job fail faster; it does not make Claude Code run non-interactively.
distractors.B: Piping 'yes' into stdin is a fragile hack that does not properly invoke non-interactive mode.
distractors.C: --interactive=false is not a real Claude Code flag; -p is the supported mechanism.

stem: Which statement best captures the difference between CLAUDE.md and a skill?

options.A: CLAUDE.md is for tool definitions; skills are for documentation
options.B: They are interchangeable; the choice is purely stylistic
options.C: Skills are always loaded; CLAUDE.md is loaded only when a matching file is edited
options.D: CLAUDE.md provides always-loaded universal standards; skills are invoked on-demand for task-specific workflows

explanation: CLAUDE.md is always loaded into context and carries universal, always-on standards. Skills are invoked on demand for specific task workflows, keeping their (often verbose) instructions out of context until needed.

distractors.A: Neither is primarily a tool-definition mechanism; both carry instructions/workflows, not tool schemas.
distractors.B: They serve distinct purposes (always-on vs on-demand), so the choice is functional, not stylistic.
distractors.C: This reverses the truth: CLAUDE.md is always loaded; skills are on-demand.

stem: Claude Code is behaving inconsistently across sessions and you suspect a stale or wrong memory file is being loaded. Which command helps you verify which memory (CLAUDE.md) files are active?

options.A: /config
options.B: /rules
options.C: /reload
options.D: /memory

explanation: The /memory command shows which memory (CLAUDE.md) files are currently loaded, letting you diagnose hierarchy issues such as a user-level file overriding or conflicting with project-level instructions.

distractors.A: /config is not the command for inspecting loaded memory files.
distractors.B: /rules is not the command used to list loaded CLAUDE.md memory files.
distractors.C: /reload is not the diagnostic command for which memory files are loaded.

stem: Your repo has Terraform under infrastructure/, React components under web/, and Python services under services/. Test files live next to the source files they cover throughout all three trees. You want testing conventions to apply to every test file regardless of location, while each language tree keeps its own conventions. What is the best configuration?

options.A: Put testing conventions in a directory CLAUDE.md at the repo root
options.B: Create a skill that developers invoke before writing any test
options.C: Put all conventions, including testing, in one large root CLAUDE.md
options.D: Use directory-level CLAUDE.md files in each language tree, plus a .claude/rules/ file with a glob like "**/*.test.*" (and equivalents) for testing conventions

explanation: Directory CLAUDE.md files are ideal when conventions are contained within a single directory tree (the per-language conventions). Path-specific .claude/rules/ with glob patterns are ideal when conventions must apply to files spread across the codebase by type (test files everywhere). Combining them matches each need.

distractors.A: A directory CLAUDE.md at the root applies to the whole tree, not selectively to test files by type.
distractors.B: A manually invoked skill is not reliable for always-on conventions and adds friction.
distractors.C: A single root CLAUDE.md loads everything for every file, wasting context and mixing unrelated conventions.

stem: You want a custom slash command that accepts a free-form argument the user types after the command name (e.g., /triage <issue-url>) and injects it into the prompt. What is the correct mechanism?

options.A: Reference $ARGUMENTS in the command's markdown body
options.B: Add context: fork to the command frontmatter
options.C: Define a JSON schema in the command file describing the argument
options.D: Add an argument-hint frontmatter key and Claude will parse the value

explanation: Custom slash commands substitute the text the user supplies into the command body via the $ARGUMENTS placeholder. This is the mechanism for passing free-form input to a command.

distractors.B: context: fork controls isolated execution context; it does not capture command arguments.
distractors.C: Commands do not take a JSON argument schema; $ARGUMENTS is the substitution mechanism.
distractors.D: argument-hint is a skill frontmatter affordance that prompts for parameters; it is not how commands receive substituted argument text.

stem: You are authoring a codebase-analysis skill that produces hundreds of lines of exploratory output. You want that output kept out of the main conversation so it does not consume the user's context budget. Which frontmatter setting accomplishes this?

options.A: output: summary
options.B: context: fork
options.C: allowed-tools: [Read, Grep, Glob]
options.D: argument-hint: "target directory"

explanation: context: fork runs the skill in an isolated sub-agent context so its verbose output does not pollute the main conversation; only a summary returns. This is the recommended pattern for verbose/exploratory skills.

distractors.A: output: summary is not a real frontmatter key.
distractors.C: allowed-tools restricts which tools the skill may use; it does not isolate context.
distractors.D: argument-hint prompts for a required parameter; it has no effect on context isolation.

stem: A skill is supposed to operate read-only (it should never modify files), but it occasionally writes files when the model decides to. What is the most reliable way to guarantee it cannot write?

options.A: Set context: fork so writes happen in an isolated context
options.B: Set allowed-tools to a read-only set (e.g., Read, Grep, Glob) so write tools are unavailable
options.C: Add a sentence to the skill body saying 'do not modify any files'
options.D: Add an argument-hint warning the user that the skill is read-only

explanation: allowed-tools restricts the tools available during skill execution. Limiting it to read-only tools makes write/edit tools simply unavailable, giving a deterministic guarantee rather than relying on the model to comply with prose.

distractors.A: context: fork isolates context but does not prevent the forked context from writing files.
distractors.C: Prose instructions are probabilistic; the model can still choose to write despite them.
distractors.D: argument-hint only prompts for parameters; it does not restrict tools.

stem: Two .claude/rules/ files match the file you are editing: rules/general.md with paths ["src/**/*"] and rules/payments.md with paths ["src/payments/**/*.ts"]. The file is src/payments/charge.ts. Which rule guidance should take precedence when they conflict?

options.A: Neither; conflicting rules are both ignored
options.B: payments.md, because its glob is more specific to the file
options.C: general.md, because it was likely defined first
options.D: Whichever file is alphabetically last

explanation: When multiple path-specific rules match, the more specific glob (the narrower, deeper pattern targeting src/payments/**/*.ts) is the more targeted match and should govern that file, just as more-specific scoping wins elsewhere in the config hierarchy.

distractors.A: Matching rules are applied, not discarded; the more specific one governs on conflict.
distractors.C: Definition order is not the basis for precedence; specificity of the path match is.
distractors.D: Alphabetical ordering is not how rule precedence is determined.

stem: You want to keep your monolithic root CLAUDE.md from growing unwieldy. Standards for API conventions, testing, and deployment each live in their own markdown files under standards/. What is the recommended way to compose them into CLAUDE.md?

options.A: Convert each standards file into a separate skill and invoke them all at session start
options.B: Use @import references (e.g., @import ./standards/api-conventions.md) so each package imports only relevant standards
options.C: Move all standards into ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md so they load globally
options.D: Copy the full text of each file into CLAUDE.md and keep them in sync manually

explanation: @import lets CLAUDE.md reference external files, keeping it modular. Each package's CLAUDE.md can import only the standards relevant to its domain, avoiding a giant monolith and avoiding manual duplication.

distractors.A: Skills are on-demand and would not reliably load always-on standards at every session.
distractors.C: User-level CLAUDE.md is not shared with the team and is not the modularization mechanism.
distractors.D: Copying and manually syncing duplicates content and drifts out of date.

stem: Your CI generates unit tests, but many duplicate tests that already exist or cover trivial getters/setters. What two-part change most improves generated test quality?

options.A: Run the generator twice and keep only tests that appear both times
options.B: Lower temperature to 0 and increase max_tokens
options.C: Provide the existing test files in context and document testing standards plus valuable-test criteria in CLAUDE.md
options.D: Only run test generation on files with low code coverage

explanation: Supplying existing test files lets the generator avoid duplicating scenarios, and documenting testing standards and what counts as a valuable test in CLAUDE.md steers it away from trivial getters/setters. Both address the underlying knowledge gap.

distractors.A: Running twice does not give the model knowledge of existing tests or quality criteria.
distractors.B: Temperature and token limits do not tell the model what tests already exist or which are worth writing.
distractors.D: Coverage targeting helps prioritize but still produces duplicates and trivial tests without the missing context.

stem: You want to guarantee that whenever Claude Code edits a file under src/, the formatter runs and the file is reformatted before the change is considered done, without relying on the model to remember. Which hook fits best?

options.A: A Stop hook that returns additionalContext reminding Claude to format
options.B: A line in CLAUDE.md instructing Claude to always format after editing
options.C: A PostToolUse hook that runs the formatter on the edited file after the write completes
options.D: A PreToolUse hook that blocks all edits to src/

explanation: PostToolUse hooks fire after a tool runs and are the right place for deterministic transformations like formatting the file that was just written. This guarantees formatting without depending on the model.

distractors.A: A Stop hook returning additionalContext nudges the model but is still probabilistic, not a guaranteed transform.
distractors.B: A CLAUDE.md instruction is probabilistic; the model may forget to format.
distractors.D: PreToolUse fires before the edit and is for blocking/validation, not for formatting a not-yet-written file.

stem: Company policy forbids editing files under config/secrets/. Developers occasionally still ask Claude Code to touch them. What gives the strongest, deterministic guarantee that such edits are blocked before they happen?

options.A: A PreToolUse hook that inspects the target path and denies any Edit/Write to config/secrets/
options.B: Few-shot examples in the prompt showing Claude refusing such edits
options.C: A PostToolUse hook that reverts any change made under config/secrets/
options.D: A bold warning in CLAUDE.md telling Claude never to edit config/secrets/

explanation: PreToolUse hooks intercept a tool call before it executes and can deterministically deny it based on the target path. This blocks the edit before any change is made, which prompt-based approaches cannot guarantee.

distractors.B: Few-shot examples reduce but do not eliminate the failure rate; they are not deterministic.
distractors.C: A PostToolUse revert lets the write happen first (briefly exposing secrets) and is reactive, not preventive.
distractors.D: A CLAUDE.md warning is probabilistic and can be ignored by the model.

stem: You want a workflow where, when Claude tries to end its turn, a Stop hook checks whether the test suite passed; if not, it should feed the failing test output back to Claude and let it keep working in the SAME turn, without the hook being treated as an error. In Claude Code v2.1.163+, what is the correct hook output?

options.A: Return a PreToolUse deny decision so the turn is blocked
options.B: Return hookSpecificOutput.additionalContext containing the failing test output (within the 10,000-char cap)
options.C: Write the failures to CLAUDE.md so they load next session
options.D: Exit with a non-zero status code and print the failures to stderr

explanation: As of v2.1.163 (June 2026), Stop and SubagentStop hooks can return hookSpecificOutput.additionalContext to feed Claude context and continue the turn WITHOUT being treated as a hook error. The additionalContext is capped at 10,000 characters.

distractors.A: PreToolUse deny is the wrong hook type and is about blocking tool calls, not continuing after Stop.
distractors.C: Writing to CLAUDE.md affects future sessions, not the current turn's continuation.
distractors.D: A non-zero exit is treated as a hook error and does not cleanly feed context back to continue the turn.

stem: You are packaging your team's set of skills as a Claude Code plugin so a colleague can use them. Using the May 2026 workflow, you run `claude plugin init mytools` and place SKILL.md files in the plugin's .claude/skills directories. What is the result for the colleague?

options.A: The skills auto-load as mytools@skills-dir with no marketplace or install step
options.B: The skills must be published to a marketplace and installed before they are usable
options.C: The skills only work if added to the colleague's user-level CLAUDE.md
options.D: The colleague must copy each SKILL.md into their own ~/.claude/skills manually

explanation: As of May 2026, `claude plugin init <name>` scaffolds a plugin, and skills placed in its .claude/skills directories auto-load as <name>@skills-dir with no marketplace publish or install step required.

distractors.B: The whole point of the May 2026 change is that no marketplace or install step is needed.
distractors.C: CLAUDE.md is for instructions, not for registering plugin skills; auto-load handles registration.
distractors.D: Manual copying into ~/.claude/skills is the old, friction-heavy approach this feature removes.

stem: Your single Claude Code session writes a feature and then reviews its own diff, but it consistently rates its own code as fine and misses real bugs. What is the most effective architectural change?

options.A: Run the review in a separate reviewer subagent/instance with isolated context from the implementer
options.B: Increase the model's temperature so the review is more varied
options.C: Re-run the same session twice and merge the two reviews
options.D: Tell the same session in the prompt to 'be a harsh, skeptical reviewer'

explanation: A session that generated code is less effective at critically reviewing its own changes because it shares the implementer's assumptions and context. Using a separate reviewer subagent with isolated context removes that bias and catches more real issues.

distractors.B: Temperature changes randomness, not the structural self-review bias.
distractors.C: Re-running the same context inherits the same blind spots both times.
distractors.D: Prompting the same biased context to be 'harsh' does not remove the shared assumptions that cause blind spots.

stem: Your automated PR review runs on every push. After a developer addresses the first round and pushes again, the second run re-flags the already-fixed issues and adds new ones, spamming the PR with duplicate comments. What is the best fix?

options.A: Include the prior review findings in context and instruct Claude to report only new or still-unaddressed issues
options.B: Only review the files changed in the latest commit
options.C: Clear all context before each run so the review starts fresh
options.D: Post the full review every time and let developers ignore duplicates

explanation: Incremental reviews should carry prior findings into context and instruct Claude to report only new or still-unaddressed issues. This prevents duplicate comments while still catching newly introduced problems.

distractors.B: Reviewing only latest-commit files misses cross-file integration issues introduced by the changes.
distractors.C: Clearing context guarantees the same issues get re-flagged because the model has no memory of prior findings.
distractors.D: Spamming duplicates and relying on developers to filter degrades trust in the tool.

stem: A shared project skill named /deploy works well for the team, but you personally want a slightly different variant that does an extra dry-run step, without changing the team's behavior or affecting teammates. What is the cleanest approach?

options.A: Add context: fork to the project skill so it runs differently for you
options.B: Override the team skill by adding deploy to your ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md
options.C: Edit the project's .claude/skills/deploy/SKILL.md and only commit it locally
options.D: Create a personal variant under ~/.claude/skills/ with a different name (e.g., deploy-dryrun)

explanation: Personal skill customization is done by creating a variant in user-level ~/.claude/skills/ with a different name. This is private, does not touch the shared project skill, and avoids affecting teammates.

distractors.A: context: fork changes context isolation, not the deploy steps, and editing the shared skill affects everyone.
distractors.B: CLAUDE.md cannot redefine a skill's steps; it is not the skill mechanism.
distractors.C: Editing the committed project skill risks affecting teammates and creates messy 'do not commit' state.

stem: Your team wants every member to share a GitHub MCP server when they open the repo in Claude Code, but each member's personal access token must NOT be committed. What is the correct configuration?

options.A: Commit the token to .mcp.json so the server works immediately on clone
options.B: Add the server to each developer's user-level config and ask everyone to replicate it
options.C: Add the server to project-scoped .mcp.json and reference the token via environment variable expansion (e.g., ${GITHUB_TOKEN})
options.D: Add the server config to the project CLAUDE.md with the token inline

explanation: Project-scoped .mcp.json is committed to version control so the server is available to everyone on clone, and ${VAR} environment-variable expansion keeps each developer's token out of the repo. Each developer sets GITHUB_TOKEN locally.

distractors.A: Committing the token to .mcp.json leaks a secret into version control.
distractors.B: User-level config is not shared and requires manual replication by every member, defeating the goal.
distractors.D: CLAUDE.md is for instructions, not MCP server configuration, and inlining the token commits a secret.

stem: You need a custom `/review` slash command for your team's standard code-review workflow, and it must be available to every teammate who clones the repository. Where should you create the command file?

options.A: In `.claude/commands/` inside the project repository
options.B: In `~/.claude/commands/` in each developer's home directory
options.C: In the root `CLAUDE.md` file
options.D: In `.claude/config.json`

explanation: Project commands stored in `.claude/commands/` are version-controlled with the repository and automatically available to everyone who clones it — exactly the requirement for a shared team command.

distractors.B: `~/.claude/commands/` holds personal commands on one developer's machine; teammates would not get the command from a clone.
distractors.C: CLAUDE.md carries instructions and context, not slash-command definitions.
distractors.D: `.claude/config.json` is not a mechanism for defining slash commands.

stem: You need to restructure a monolithic application into microservices — a change spanning dozens of files and requiring service-boundary decisions. What approach should you use in Claude Code?

options.A: Use planning mode first: explore the codebase, understand dependencies, and design an approach before making any edits
options.B: Use direct execution and refactor incrementally, one piece at a time
options.C: Use direct execution with very detailed up-front instructions describing the target architecture
options.D: Start with direct execution and switch to planning mode only when the work becomes difficult

explanation: Planning mode is designed exactly for large changes with multiple possible approaches and architectural decisions. Exploring the codebase and designing the decomposition before editing avoids committing to a flawed structure across dozens of files.

distractors.B: Incremental direct execution without an up-front design risks expensive rework when early service-boundary choices prove wrong.
distractors.C: Detailed up-front instructions assume you already know the right structure, which is precisely what needs to be discovered through exploration.
distractors.D: Switching to planning only after problems appear is reactive; by then poor architectural choices may already be baked into the changes.

stem: Your CI pipeline runs the Claude Code CLI in `--print` mode with CLAUDE.md providing project context for code review, and developers find the reviews substantive. However, integrating findings is painful: Claude outputs narrative paragraphs that must be manually copied into PR comments. The team wants to automatically post each finding as a separate inline PR comment at the relevant place in code, which requires structured data with file path, line number, severity level, and suggested fix. Which approach is most effective?

options.A: Add an "Output Format for Review" section to CLAUDE.md with examples of structured findings so Claude learns the expected format from project context
options.B: Use the CLI flags `--output-format json` and `--json-schema` to enforce structured findings, then parse the output and post inline comments via the GitHub API
options.C: Include explicit formatting instructions in the review prompt requiring each finding to follow a parseable template like `[FILE:path] [LINE:n] [SEVERITY:level] ...`
options.D: Keep the narrative review format but add a second Claude pass that converts it into a structured JSON summary of findings

explanation: `--output-format json` combined with `--json-schema` enforces structured output at the CLI level, guaranteeing well-formed JSON with the required fields (file path, line number, severity, suggested fix) that can be reliably parsed and posted as inline PR comments via the GitHub API. It uses built-in CLI capabilities designed specifically for structured output, rather than hoping the model formats correctly.

distractors.A: CLAUDE.md examples are probabilistic guidance; the model can still deviate, and any deviation breaks automated parsing.
distractors.C: A prompt-level text template is also probabilistic and fragile — a single malformed line breaks the parser, with no enforcement mechanism behind it.
distractors.D: A second conversion pass adds cost and latency, and the lossy narrative-to-JSON conversion can drop or distort findings.

stem: You need to add Slack as a new notification channel. The existing codebase has clear, established patterns for email, SMS, and push channels. However, Slack's API offers fundamentally different integration approaches—incoming webhooks (simple, one-way), bot tokens (delivery confirmation and programmatic control), or Slack Apps (two-way events, requires workspace approval). Your task says "add Slack support" without specifying the integration method or requiring advanced features like delivery tracking. How should you approach this task in Claude Code?

options.A: Start in direct execution mode using incoming webhooks to match the existing one-way notification pattern.
options.B: Switch to planning mode to explore the integration options and their architectural implications, then present a recommendation before implementation.
options.C: Start in direct execution mode by scaffolding a Slack channel class using the existing patterns, deferring the integration-method decision until later.
options.D: Start in direct execution mode using a bot-token approach to ensure delivery confirmation is possible.

explanation: Slack integration has multiple valid approaches with significantly different architectural implications, and the requirements are ambiguous. Planning mode lets you safely evaluate the trade-offs among webhooks, bot tokens, and Slack Apps and align on an approach before committing to implementation.

distractors.A: Choosing webhooks by analogy to existing channels silently forecloses delivery tracking and two-way features without ever surfacing the decision to stakeholders.
distractors.C: Scaffolding before choosing an integration method bakes in assumptions—the class interface differs meaningfully between webhooks, bot tokens, and Slack Apps.
distractors.D: Defaulting to bot tokens assumes a requirement (delivery confirmation) that was never stated, adding complexity that may be unnecessary.

stem: Your CLAUDE.md file has grown to 400+ lines containing coding standards, testing conventions, a detailed PR review checklist, deployment instructions, and database migration procedures. You want Claude to always follow the coding standards and testing conventions, but apply the PR review, deployment, and migration guidance only when actually doing those tasks. Which restructuring approach is most effective?

options.A: Move all guidance into separate skill files organized by workflow type, leaving only a brief project description in CLAUDE.md.
options.B: Keep everything in CLAUDE.md but use `@import` syntax to organize the content into separately maintained files by category.
options.C: Split CLAUDE.md into files under `.claude/rules/` with path-bound glob patterns so each rule loads only for the relevant file types.
options.D: Keep the universal standards in CLAUDE.md and create skills for the workflow-specific guidance (PR review, deployment, migrations) with trigger keywords.

explanation: CLAUDE.md content loads in every session, which is exactly right for coding standards and testing conventions that must always apply. Skills are invoked on demand when Claude detects relevant triggers—ideal for workflow-specific guidance like PR review, deployment, and migrations that would otherwise waste always-on context.

distractors.A: Moving the universal standards into skills means they no longer load automatically in every session, so coding and testing conventions would only apply when a skill happens to be invoked.
distractors.B: `@import` improves maintainability but everything imported still loads into every session, so the workflow-specific content keeps consuming context on unrelated tasks.
distractors.C: Path-bound rules activate by file path, but PR review, deployment, and migrations are workflows, not file types—glob patterns are the wrong activation trigger for them.

stem: You're tasked with restructuring your team's monolithic application into microservices. The work impacts dozens of files and requires decisions about service boundaries and module dependencies. Which Claude Code approach should you choose?

options.A: Switch to planning mode to explore the codebase, understand the dependencies, and design the implementation approach before making any changes.
options.B: Start in direct execution mode and switch to planning only after encountering unexpected complexity during implementation.
options.C: Start in direct execution mode and make incremental changes, letting the implementation reveal natural service boundaries.
options.D: Use direct execution mode with detailed upfront instructions that specify the structure of each service.

explanation: Planning mode is the right strategy for complex architectural restructuring like splitting a monolith: it allows safe, read-only exploration and informed decisions about service boundaries before committing to expensive changes across dozens of files.

distractors.B: Reactive planning after complexity bites means early changes were made without understanding dependencies, likely requiring rework of already-modified files.
distractors.C: Letting implementation reveal boundaries inverts the process—service boundaries are exactly the kind of cross-cutting decision that must be designed before code moves.
distractors.D: Detailed upfront instructions in execution mode assume you already know the right structure; without exploration, those instructions encode guesses about dependencies.

stem: Your team has used Claude Code for months. Recently, three developers report that Claude follows the guidance "always include comprehensive error handling," but a fourth developer who just joined says Claude does not follow it. All four work in the same repo and have up-to-date code. What is the most likely cause and fix?

options.A: The guidance lives in the original developers' user-level `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` files, not in the project-level `.claude/CLAUDE.md`; move the instruction to the project-level file so all team members receive it.
options.B: The new developer's `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` contains conflicting instructions overriding the project settings; they should delete the conflicting section.
options.C: Claude Code learns per-user preferences over time, so the new developer must repeat the requirement until Claude "remembers" it.
options.D: Claude Code caches CLAUDE.md after the first read, so the original developers are using cached versions; everyone should clear the Claude Code cache.

explanation: If the guidance was added only to the original developers' user-level configs and never to the project-level `.claude/CLAUDE.md`, new team members will not receive it even with up-to-date code. Moving it to the project-level file—which is version-controlled and loaded for everyone—ensures all current and future members get it automatically.

distractors.B: Conflicting personal instructions are possible but less likely as a first hypothesis: the new developer presumably has a fresh, near-empty user config, and nothing suggests they added contradicting rules.
distractors.C: Claude Code does not implicitly learn preferences over time; persistent guidance comes from memory files like CLAUDE.md, not gradual reinforcement.
distractors.D: Claude Code reads memory files at session start rather than serving stale long-term caches; cache-clearing is not the documented remedy for inconsistent guidance.

stem: You find that including 2–3 full endpoint implementation examples as context significantly improves consistency when generating new API endpoints. However, this context is useful only when creating new endpoints—not when debugging, reviewing code, or doing other work in the API directory. Which configuration approach is most effective?

options.A: Add the endpoint examples and pattern documentation to the project CLAUDE.md so they are always available.
options.B: Manually reference the endpoint examples in every generation request by copying the code into the prompt.
options.C: Configure path-specific rules in `.claude/rules/api/` that include the endpoint examples and activate when working in the API directory.
options.D: Create a skill that references the endpoint examples and contains pattern-following instructions, invoked on demand via a slash command.

explanation: A skill invoked on demand loads the bulky example context only for the task that needs it—generating new endpoints—and not during debugging, review, or other API-directory work. This keeps the main context clean while preserving high-quality, pattern-consistent generation when invoked.

distractors.A: CLAUDE.md content loads in every session, so several full endpoint implementations would permanently consume context even for tasks that never need them.
distractors.B: Manual copy-paste works but is error-prone and unscalable—every developer must remember to do it, and versions of the examples drift.
distractors.C: Path-bound rules activate for ANY work in the API directory, including debugging and review where the examples are explicitly not wanted; the trigger is task type, not file path.

stem: Your team created a `/migration` skill that generates database migration files, taking the migration name via `$ARGUMENTS`. In production you observe three issues: (1) developers often run the skill without arguments, causing poorly named files, (2) the skill sometimes uses database schema details from unrelated prior conversations, and (3) a developer accidentally ran destructive test cleanup because the skill had broad tool access. Which configuration approach fixes all three problems?

options.A: Use positional parameters `$1` and `$2` instead of `$ARGUMENTS` to enforce specific inputs, include explicit schema file references via `@` syntax for context control, and add a frontmatter description warning about destructive operations.
options.B: Add `argument-hint` in the frontmatter to prompt for the required parameter, use `context: fork` to isolate execution from the main conversation, and restrict `allowed-tools` to file-write operations.
options.C: Split the skill into `/migration-create` and `/migration-apply`, add validation instructions to request the migration name if missing, and use different `allowed-tools` scopes for each.
options.D: Add validation instructions in SKILL.md to ensure `$ARGUMENTS` is a valid name, add prompts telling the model to ignore prior conversation context, and list prohibited operations to avoid.

explanation: Each problem maps to a dedicated configuration feature: `argument-hint` surfaces the expected parameter at invocation time, reducing missing-argument runs; `context: fork` isolates execution so stale schema details from prior conversation cannot leak in; and `allowed-tools` deterministically constrains the skill to safe file-writing operations, preventing destructive actions.

distractors.A: Positional parameters do not stop developers from omitting arguments, `@` references add context but do not exclude polluted conversation history, and a frontmatter warning is advisory—nothing deterministically blocks destructive tool use.
distractors.C: Splitting the skill restructures the workflow but argument omission, context leakage, and tool scoping must still be solved within each new skill—the split itself fixes nothing.
distractors.D: All three fixes here are prompt-level instructions the model may not follow; instructions to 'ignore prior context' and 'avoid prohibited operations' are probabilistic, unlike fork isolation and tool restriction.

stem: You want to create a custom slash command `/review` that runs your team's standard code review checklist. It should be available to every developer when they clone or update the repository. Where should you create the command file?

options.A: In `~/.claude/commands/` in each developer's home directory.
options.B: In the project repository under `.claude/commands/`.
options.C: In `.claude/config.json` as an array of commands.
options.D: In the root project CLAUDE.md.

explanation: Custom slash commands placed under `.claude/commands/` inside the project repository are version-controlled and automatically available to every developer who clones or updates the repo—this is the intended location for shared, project-level commands.

distractors.A: Home-directory commands are personal: each developer would have to copy the file manually, and updates would not propagate via the repo.
distractors.C: Slash commands are defined as Markdown files in a commands directory, not as an array inside a config JSON file.
distractors.D: CLAUDE.md holds persistent instructions and context; it is not a mechanism for defining slash commands.

stem: Your team's CLAUDE.md grew beyond 500 lines, mixing TypeScript conventions, testing guidance, API patterns, and deployment procedures. Developers find it hard to locate and update the right sections. What approach does Claude Code support for organizing project-level instructions into focused topical modules?

options.A: Define a `.claude/config.yaml` mapping file patterns to specific sections inside CLAUDE.md.
options.B: Create separate Markdown files in `.claude/rules/`, each covering one topic (e.g., `testing.md`, `api-conventions.md`).
options.C: Split the instructions into README.md files in relevant subdirectories that Claude automatically loads as instructions.
options.D: Create multiple files named CLAUDE.md at different levels of the directory tree, each overriding its parent's instructions.

explanation: Claude Code supports a `.claude/rules/` directory where teams create separate Markdown files for topical guidance (e.g., `testing.md`, `api-conventions.md`), turning one unwieldy CLAUDE.md into focused, independently maintainable modules.

distractors.A: There is no `.claude/config.yaml` mechanism that maps file patterns to CLAUDE.md sections.
distractors.C: README.md files are ordinary documentation; Claude Code does not automatically load them as instruction memory.
distractors.D: Nested CLAUDE.md files add directory-scoped context but they do not override parent instructions, and scattering overrides is not the supported modularization pattern for topical organization.
